NEVER add ROUND(), division, or other transformations to force a formula to match the user's stated value. Always compute or construct the formula based on the actual data and correct logic. The user's stated value may be approximate, rounded, or wrong. The scoring system checks the actual computed result, not the user's stated expectation.
    10. Do NOT give up if data appears missing at first glance. Look more carefully: check all sheets, check whether row 1 vs row 2 is the header, check for merged cells, check if the data layout differs from your assumption. Print more rows and columns before concluding data is absent.
    11. For conditional formatting tasks: in addition to (or instead of) adding openpyxl CF rules, compute the condition in Python and directly set cell fills using PatternFill. CF rules alone may not produce values that survive recalculation scoring. Apply the fill directly: cell.fill = PatternFill(start_color="FFFF00", end_color="FFFF00", fill_type="solid").
    12. Before modifying cell values, check for pre-existing data that already satisfies the condition. For example, if the task says "append '-Fuel' to column I", first check if the cell already ends with '-Fuel' to avoid double-appending. Read and print the current values before deciding what to change. Do NOT overwrite existing labels, headers, or content that should be preserved unless the instruction explicitly asks you to change them.
    13. Save to /sandbox/output/output_spreadsheet/ with the SAME filename as the input.
    14. After saving, call recalculate() on the output file to evaluate any written formulas.
    15. IMPORTANT: recalculate() may change sheet names (e.g., "Sheet1" → "Ark1" due to LibreOffice locale). After recalculate, reload the file and check that sheet names still match the original. If a sheet was renamed, rename it back to the original name, save again, and do NOT call recalculate a second time.
    16. Verify the output by reloading the saved file (data_only=True after recalculation) and printing EVERY target cell/range mentioned in the instruction. Confirm:
       a. No target cells are None or blank when they should have values.
       b. If any target cells are None after recalculation, the formula is likely incompatible with LibreOffice. DO NOT SUBMIT — instead, debug the formula (print it, check syntax, try alternative approaches), and if the formula still fails, fall back to computing the result in Python and writing the static value directly. Re-save, re-recalculate, and re-verify.
       c. String values have no unintended leading/trailing whitespace.
       d. Numeric values are plausible (sanity-check against the input data).
       e. All sheets expected in the output actually exist with correct names.
       f. The number of output rows/columns matches what the instruction requires.
       g. Verify the EXACT target column — if the instruction says column H, confirm you wrote to column H (column index 8), not column F or another column. Count columns explicitly using openpyxl.utils.column_index_from_string().
       h. If formulas evaluate to #N/A, #VALUE!, or #REF!, the formula is wrong. Do NOT submit — debug the formula syntax, fix it, re-save, re-recalculate, and re-verify.
       i. If string output is expected, compare your output against any example values in the spreadsheet to ensure exact format match (punctuation, spacing, wording). Even small differences like "diff" vs "difference" will cause a mismatch.
    17. If verification reveals mismatches, debug and fix before submitting. Re-save, re-recalculate, and re-verify. NEVER submit when you know target cells are None or formulas failed to evaluate.
    18. When the task involves extracting or filtering data, double-check the filtering condition against the actual data. Print the rows that match and those that don't to confirm the filter is correct. If the instruction says "values corresponding to rows where there's an 'X'", verify which rows have 'X' and in which column.
    19. When the task involves complex formula logic (VLOOKUP with conditions, INDEX/MATCH with multiple criteria, array formulas), and the formula returns None or errors after recalculation, fall back to implementing the logic in Python. Compute the values and write them directly to cells as static values. This is acceptable and preferable to submitting None values.
    20. When writing formulas that reference named ranges or structured references (e.g., Data[], Table1[Column]), verify these names exist in the workbook. If they don't exist in the openpyxl representation, use explicit cell ranges instead.'}
